---
title: "Getting Started with Claude Code"
subtitle: "A practical guide for IPA Colombia Research Analysts"
author:
  - name: "Diego Quintero Mogollon"
    email: "dquintero@poverty-action.org"
    affiliation: "IPA Colombia"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: false
    number-sections: false
    embed-resources: true
    theme:
      light: cosmo
      dark: darkly
    smooth-scroll: true
    code-copy: true
    link-external-newwindow: true
    css: styles.css
execute:
  echo: false
  eval: false
---

```{=html}
<div class="hero-banner">
  <div class="hero-content">
    <span class="beta-badge">BETA v0.1</span>
    <h1 class="hero-title">Learn Claude Code<br>for Stata Research</h1>
    <p class="hero-subtitle">
      Go from zero to productive with an AI coding assistant.<br>
      No prior experience with VS Code or Claude Code required.
    </p>
    <div class="hero-badges">
      <span class="badge-item">📖 5 Modules</span>
      <span class="badge-item">🔒 Data Safety</span>
      <span class="badge-item">🎯 2 Exercises</span>
      <span class="badge-item">⏱️ 50–75 min</span>
    </div>
  </div>
</div>

<div class="feedback-banner">
  <p>
    <strong>This guide is in beta.</strong>
    This is my first attempt at introducing Claude Code to IPA research analysts.
    I built it entirely with Claude Code itself, so it may contain errors or rough edges.
    Feedback, corrections, and suggestions are very welcome — they will directly shape the next version.
    Please reach out to me at <a href="mailto:dquintero@poverty-action.org">dquintero@poverty-action.org</a>
    or open an issue on <a href="https://github.com/Quintero-DiegoIPA/ipa-claude-code-intro/issues">GitHub</a>.
    <br><em>— Diego Quintero Mogoll&oacute;n, IPA Colombia</em>
  </p>
</div>
```

::: {.callout-important appearance="simple"}
This guide complements the [IPA AI Usage Guidelines](https://ipastorage.box.com/s/mvr67ygvz1y3v8qmgjey67lk7msmyeks). Read those first for the full data classification and approval framework.
:::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**This is the tip of the iceberg.** This guide is a very initial introduction to Claude Code, focused on getting IPA research analysts up and running. For a much deeper dive into AI-assisted workflows, prompting strategies, and advanced use cases, I have found the [Chris Blattman AI guide](https://claudeblattman.com/) incredibly valuable — it covers everything from basic setup to building custom skills and automating entire workflows. I highly recommend it as a next step after this guide.
:::

::: {.module-nav}
Five modules. Work through them in order, or jump to what you need.
:::

::: {.panel-tabset .module-tabs}

## 1. Setup (~15 min)

::::: {.two-col}
:::: {.col-left}

### What You Need

:::{.card}
#### Must Have
- Stata 17 or later, installed and licensed
- Node.js 18 or later ([download](https://nodejs.org/))
- An Anthropic API key **or** a Claude Max/Pro account
:::

:::{.card}
#### Good to Have
- A GitHub account ([sign up](https://github.com/))
- A project repository cloned to your machine
- Familiarity with IPA/DIME Stata coding standards
:::

::::
:::: {.col-right}

### What Is GitHub?

GitHub is a platform where teams store and collaborate on code. Think of it as a shared folder with superpowers: it keeps a complete history of every change, lets multiple people work on the same files without overwriting each other, and provides a backup of all project code outside your local machine.

At IPA, each project has a **repository** (repo) on GitHub. A repository is a folder that contains all the do-files, documentation, and configuration for one project. The code lives on GitHub, and each analyst **clones** (downloads) a copy to their own computer to work on.

::::
:::::

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### Repository
A project folder on GitHub. Contains do-files, documentation, and configs. Each project gets one repo.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### Clone
Download a copy of the repo to your computer. You edit your local copy, then push changes back.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### Commit & Push
Save a snapshot of your changes (commit) and upload them to GitHub (push) so the team can see them.
:::
::::
:::::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
You do not need to be a Git expert. The three concepts above are enough to get started. Claude Code can help with Git commands when you need them.
:::

---

### Project Folder Structure

IPA projects should follow a standardized folder structure. The official template is available at [PovertyAction/ipa-stata-template](https://github.com/PovertyAction/ipa-stata-template) on GitHub. To start a new project, go to that repository and click **"Use this template"** to create your own copy.

::::: {.two-col}
:::: {.col-left}

```
my-project/
├── do_files/           # Stata scripts
│   └── 00_run.do       # Master do-file
├── data/               # Raw, cleaned, final
├── outputs/            # Tables and figures
│   ├── tables/
│   └── figures/
├── logs/               # Stata execution logs
├── ado/                # Local Stata packages
├── documentation/      # Project guides
├── .config/            # Configuration files
├── .env                # Local Stata path
└── .here               # Root marker
```

::::
:::: {.col-right}

The template supports three tiers:

:::{.card}
#### Tier 1: Minimal
Git + Stata only. Clone the template, configure `.env`, start writing do-files.
:::

:::{.card}
#### Tier 2: Task Runner
Add `just` for automation. Run scripts with `just stata-run` instead of opening Stata manually.
:::

:::{.card}
#### Tier 3: Full
Python tools, pre-commit hooks, and Claude Code integration with `CLAUDE.md` and skills.
:::

::::
:::::

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
If you already have a project repo, you do not need to start over. The folder structure above is a reference. Ask your project lead how your repo is organized.
:::

---

### VS Code: Your New Workspace

Visual Studio Code (VS Code) is a free code editor where Claude Code runs. Download it from [code.visualstudio.com](https://code.visualstudio.com/).

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Sidebar
Shows your project folder structure. Toggle with `Ctrl+B`. Click any file to open it.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Editor
The main area where files open as tabs. Split the view to compare two files side by side.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Terminal
A command line built into VS Code. This is where Claude Code runs. Toggle with `` Ctrl+` ``.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Extensions
A marketplace for add-ons. Open with `Ctrl+Shift+X`.
:::
::::
:::::

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### Windows Shortcuts

| Action | Shortcut |
|--------|----------|
| Open a folder | `Ctrl+K`, then `Ctrl+O` |
| Open the terminal | `` Ctrl+` `` |
| Search across all files | `Ctrl+Shift+F` |

#### macOS Shortcuts

| Action | Shortcut |
|--------|----------|
| Open a folder | `Cmd+O` |
| Open the terminal | `` Ctrl+` `` |
| Search across all files | `Cmd+Shift+F` |
:::

**Extensions to install** (run in the VS Code terminal):

```bash
code --install-extension kylebarron.stata-enhanced
code --install-extension anthropic.claude-code
code --install-extension eamodio.gitlens
```

---

### Install and Authenticate Claude Code

::::: {.two-col}
:::: {.col-left}

**Step 1: Install.** Open the VS Code terminal (`` Ctrl+` ``):

```bash
npm install -g @anthropic-ai/claude-code
```

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Permissions error on Windows? Reopen VS Code as Administrator. On macOS, prefix with `sudo`.
:::

**Step 2: Launch and verify.**

```bash
cd /path/to/your/project
claude
```

Type `What project is this?` — Claude Code reads `CLAUDE.md` on startup and should describe your project.

::::
:::: {.col-right}

**Step 2: Authenticate.** Two options:

::: {.panel-tabset}
##### API Key (Pay Per Use)

Standard for IPA projects with shared billing. Ask your project lead for the key.

::: {.panel-tabset}
###### Windows (PowerShell)

```powershell
[System.Environment]::SetEnvironmentVariable(
  "ANTHROPIC_API_KEY",
  "sk-ant-your-key-here", "User")
```

Restart VS Code after running this.

###### macOS / Linux

Add to `~/.zshrc` or `~/.bashrc`:

```bash
export ANTHROPIC_API_KEY="sk-ant-your-key-here"
```

Then restart your terminal.
:::

##### Claude Account (Max / Pro)

Launch Claude Code and follow the browser login prompt. No environment variable needed. Session token stored locally after first login.
:::

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
**Keep credentials private.** Never paste your API key into a shared document, Slack message, or Git commit. The [IPA AI Usage Guidelines](https://ipastorage.box.com/s/mvr67ygvz1y3v8qmgjey67lk7msmyeks) list API keys and authentication tokens as **prohibited** from sharing with AI tools.
:::

::::
:::::


## 2. Concepts (~10 min)

### What Is Claude Code?

Claude Code is an AI coding assistant built by Anthropic. It runs inside the VS Code terminal as a command-line tool. Unlike browser-based chatbots, it reads the files in your project folder, understands the structure, and can write or edit code directly on your machine.

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### 🗂️ Project Context
Reads your files automatically. Sees do-files, configs, and docs. No copy-pasting into a chat window.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### ✏️ Direct Editing
Creates, modifies, and deletes files in your project. Asks for your approval before making changes.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-4}
:::{.card}
### 📋 Instructions
Follows rules in a `CLAUDE.md` file at your project root. Coding standards, conventions, and guardrails.
:::
::::
:::::

---

### What Claude Code Can and Cannot Do

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-safe}
### Can Do
- Write new do-files following IPA/DIME standards
- Debug errors by reading Stata log files
- Generate LaTeX tables with `esttab` or `outreg2`
- Explain existing code in plain language
- Review do-files for coding standard violations
- Help with Git commands
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Cannot Do
- Access or read data containing PII
- Replace your judgment on research design
- Run Stata by itself (uses `just` commands)
- Access Box, email, or external systems
- Guarantee correct statistical methodology
- Remember across separate sessions
:::
::::
:::::

---

### How Claude Code Knows Your Project

Claude Code does not start from zero each time. Three mechanisms give it persistent knowledge.

::::: {.two-col}
:::: {.col-left}

**CLAUDE.md: The project briefing.** Think of it as the document you would write for a new research analyst on their first day.

```yaml
# CLAUDE.md — SpC Project

## What This Project Is
SpC 3.0 = Sanar para Crecer 3.0 (HtG)
A large-scale caregiver education program
implemented by ICBF in Colombia.

## Project-Specific Rules
- PII: never read or display raw data files.
- Do not use cap / capture except for cap mkdir.
- Path globals defined in 0_paths.do.
```

This file is checked into Git. Every team member shares the same instructions.

::::
:::: {.col-right}

**Skills: The reference manuals.** The `.claude/skills/` directory contains specialized instruction sets.

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### Stata Skill
IPA/DIME coding standards, do-file headers, path conventions, missing value handling.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### IPA Writing Skill
Style rules for IPA content: active voice, acronym conventions, citation format.
:::
::::
:::::

Claude Code loads the relevant skill automatically. Ask it to write a do-file and it applies the Stata skill.

> **Mental model:** `CLAUDE.md` is the briefing for a new RA. Skills are the reference manuals on their desk.

**Settings and permissions.** `.claude/settings.json` controls which commands Claude Code can run without asking. Maintained by the project lead.

::::
:::::


## 3. Data Safety (~10 min)

:::{.callout-danger appearance="simple"}
**This is the most important module.** Read the full [IPA AI Usage Guidelines](https://ipastorage.box.com/s/mvr67ygvz1y3v8qmgjey67lk7msmyeks) before using Claude Code on any project with human subjects data. What follows is a summary tailored to Claude Code.
:::

### IPA Data Classification

IPA uses a four-tier data classification system. **Before using any AI tool, classify the data you plan to work with.**

| Classification | Examples | Claude Code? |
|---|---|---|
| **PUBLIC** | Published papers, public blog posts, anonymized published datasets | ✅ Yes |
| **INTERNAL** | Project documentation, do-files, draft documents without sensitive content | ✅ Yes (IPA-approved tools) |
| **CONFIDENTIAL** | Datasets with PII (names, cedula), unpublished research data, embargoed findings | ⚠️ Not with Claude Code. Use Azure OpenAI or local LLMs |
| **HIGHLY CONFIDENTIAL** | Health data, biometric data, children's sensitive data | 🛑 Contact MIST first |

Claude Code sends your messages and file contents to Anthropic's servers. This makes it appropriate for PUBLIC and INTERNAL data. For CONFIDENTIAL data containing PII, IPA's approved pathways are Azure OpenAI (via API) and local/offline LLMs.

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Where does Claude Code fit?** It uses either an Anthropic API key or a Claude Max/Pro account. Both send data to Anthropic's cloud. Under IPA guidelines, Claude Code follows the same rules as the Claude institutional license: approved for PUBLIC and INTERNAL data, not approved for PII or data restricted by contracts. When in doubt, check the [full guidelines](https://ipastorage.box.com/s/mvr67ygvz1y3v8qmgjey67lk7msmyeks) or contact MIST.
:::

---

### The Four Rules for Claude Code

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Rule 1: No PII in Prompts
Do not type or paste names, cedula numbers, addresses, phone numbers, or participant IDs. Messages are sent to Anthropic's servers.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Rule 2: No Raw Data Files
Never ask Claude Code to open files in `data/raw/` or encrypted vaults. Work only with do-files, logs, and de-identified outputs.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Rule 3: No Individual-Level Output
Do not ask for rows of data. Even de-identified data can become re-identifiable in small samples. Ask it to write code that produces summaries.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card .card-danger}
### Rule 4: Review Before Committing
Check every file Claude Code creates or modifies. Confirm no PII leaked into comments, labels, or hard-coded values. Check that no API keys appear.
:::
::::
:::::

---

### What You Can Never Share with AI Tools

Regardless of the tool, IPA prohibits sharing these without specific use-case approval:

- **PII from research participants**: names, addresses, phone numbers, ID numbers, identifiable photos or audio
- **Sensitive Personal Information (SPI)**: health data, biometric data, sexual orientation, political opinions, criminal history, data about children under 13
- **IPA credentials**: passwords, API keys, authentication tokens, encryption keys
- **Contractually restricted data**: information under NDAs, donor restrictions, embargoed findings

::: {.callout-warning appearance="simple"}
**If your dataset contains ANY SPI, the entire dataset is classified as HIGHLY CONFIDENTIAL**, even if most fields are regular PII. Contact MIST before using any AI tool.
:::

---

### Safe vs. Unsafe

> Before sending a prompt, ask: "Does this require Claude Code to see individual-level data or personal details?" If yes, rephrase to focus on the **code**, not the **data**.

| Scenario | Verdict |
|----------|---------|
| "Write a do-file that cleans survey data following this structure" | ✅ Safe (code, no data) |
| "Clean this survey data" | ❌ Unsafe (requires reading data) |
| "Why is my merge failing? Here is the log" | ✅ Safe (logs have aggregates) |
| "Show me the first 20 rows of the baseline dataset" | ❌ Unsafe (individual-level data) |
| "Debug this r(111) error in my do-file" | ✅ Safe (reads code, not data) |
| "Summarize attendance for Maria Garcia in Centro Zonal Norte" | ❌ Unsafe (names + location = PII) |

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Using AI for code generation with non-sensitive data is pre-approved** under IPA guidelines. Writing do-files, debugging Stata errors, and generating LaTeX tables are all standard, approved uses of Claude Code.
:::


## 4. Prompting (~10 min)

### The Single Most Important Idea

:::{.callout-important appearance="simple"}
**Great plans produce great outputs. Poor prompts produce poor, uncontrolled, random outputs.** The quality of what Claude Code delivers is directly proportional to the clarity and specificity of what you ask for. This is the one thing to internalize before anything else.
:::

### Use Plan Mode for Anything Non-Trivial

For any task that involves more than a quick question or a one-line edit, start by asking Claude Code to **plan before it acts**. Type `/plan` or ask it to "plan this first" before making changes.

Plan mode forces Claude Code to outline its approach and get your approval before writing code. This matters because:

- You catch misunderstandings **before** Claude Code edits 10 files in the wrong direction
- You stay in control of the analytical approach
- The plan itself becomes a record of what was done and why

::::: {.two-col}
:::: {.col-left}

:::{.card .card-danger}
#### Without Planning
```
Write me an attrition analysis do-file
```
Claude Code guesses the specification, variable names, and output format. You spend more time fixing than you saved.
:::

::::
:::: {.col-right}

:::{.card .card-safe}
#### With Planning
```
I need an attrition analysis. Use plan mode.
The outcome is a binary attrition indicator.
Regress on treatment with strata FE and
clustered SEs at the UDS level. Export with
esttab to LaTeX.
```
Claude Code outlines its plan. You approve, adjust, or redirect before any code is written.
:::

::::
:::::

---

### Five Patterns for Effective Prompts

**Pattern 1: Describe the output, not the steps**

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### ❌ Weak

```
Use regress, then outreg2, then add a title, then save as .tex
```

#### ✅ Strong

```
Run an ITT regression of FFMQ on treatment with strata fixed effects
and SEs clustered at the UDS level. Export to LaTeX using esttab.
```
:::

**Pattern 2: Reference project conventions**

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### ❌ Weak

```
Make a nice table with the results
```

#### ✅ Strong

```
Export using the esttab pattern in CLAUDE_DELIVERABLES_GUIDE.md.
Match the column structure of the attrition tables in outputs/tables/.
```
:::

**Pattern 3: Provide context about errors**

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### ❌ Weak

```
I got r(111), please fix
```

#### ✅ Strong

```
I ran my attrition analysis and got r(111). Here are the
last 30 lines of the log: [paste log excerpt].
The error is on the line referencing `treatment_status`.
```
:::

**Pattern 4: Ask for explanations alongside code**

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### ❌ Weak

```
Write the merge command
```

#### ✅ Strong

```
Write the merge command and explain why you chose 1:1 vs m:1.
Also explain what the assert results mean.
```
:::

**Pattern 5: Iterate, do not start over**

::: {.panel-tabset}
#### ❌ Weak

```
[rewrites the entire prompt from scratch]
```

#### ✅ Strong

```
The table looks right. Add baseline controls as a second column
and include the F-test for joint significance at the bottom.
```
:::

Claude Code remembers the conversation. Build on its output rather than starting over.

::: {.callout-tip appearance="simple"}
**Go deeper on prompting.** The [Blattman guide on prompting](https://claudeblattman.com/essentials/prompting) covers this topic in much more depth: how to structure complex requests, the Prompt-Plan-Review loop, and strategies for getting consistent results. I found it extremely helpful for improving my own prompts — I recommend reading it after completing this guide.
:::


## 5. Practice (~25 min)

### Exercise 1: Debug a Stata Error

:::{.exercise-banner}
**Hands-on** · 10–15 minutes · Uses Stata's built-in `auto` dataset
:::

**Scenario:** You wrote a do-file that uses Stata's built-in `auto` dataset, but it has an error. The log shows:

```stata
. sysuse auto, clear
(1978 automobile data)

. * Summary stats by car origin
. gen high_mileage = (mpg > 25)

. regress high_mileage fuel_economy weight foreign, robust
variable fuel_economy not found
r(111);

end of do-file
r(111);
```

**Your task:**

1. Open Claude Code in any project folder (`claude` in the terminal)
2. Paste the log and ask: *"I got this r(111) error. What is wrong and how do I fix it?"*
3. Read the diagnosis. Does Claude Code identify that `fuel_economy` does not exist in the `auto` dataset and that `mpg` is the correct variable?
4. Follow up: *"What other fuel-related variables exist in sysuse auto?"*
5. Evaluate the proposed fix

**What to look for:**

- Does it correctly identify `r(111)` as "variable not found"?
- Does it suggest the right replacement variable (`mpg`)?
- Does it explain why (the `auto` dataset uses `mpg`, not `fuel_economy`)?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" appearance="simple"}
## Reveal Answer

The variable `fuel_economy` does not exist in the `auto` dataset. The correct variable is `mpg` (miles per gallon). Claude Code should suggest replacing `fuel_economy` with `mpg` in the regression command. This is a common error pattern: using a variable name that seems logical but does not match the actual dataset. The fix is `regress high_mileage mpg weight foreign, robust`.
:::

---

### Exercise 2: Write a Summary Statistics Do-File

:::{.exercise-banner}
**Hands-on** · 15–20 minutes · Uses Stata's built-in `auto` dataset
:::

**Scenario:** You need a summary statistics table comparing domestic and foreign cars using Stata's built-in `auto` dataset.

**Step 1: Write the prompt.** Use this specification (remember Pattern 1 and plan mode):

::::: {.two-col}
:::: {.col-left}

**Variables:**

- `price` (car price in USD)
- `mpg` (miles per gallon)
- `weight` (car weight in lbs)
- `length` (car length in inches)
- `headroom` (headroom in inches)

::::
:::: {.col-right}

**Requirements:**

- Breakdown by origin (`foreign`: 0 = Domestic, 1 = Foreign)
- Mean, SD, N, and t-test p-value for difference
- Use `sysuse auto` as the dataset
- Save the do-file as `exercise_sumstats.do`
- Ask Claude Code to use plan mode first

::::
:::::

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
**Hint:** Try prompting like this: *"Use plan mode. I need a do-file that produces a summary statistics table from sysuse auto, comparing domestic vs foreign cars. Variables: price, mpg, weight, length, headroom. Show mean, SD, N, and a t-test p-value for each. Save as exercise_sumstats.do."*
:::

**Step 2: Review the output** against this checklist:

- [ ] Does it load `sysuse auto, clear`?
- [ ] Does it produce stats by `foreign` (0/1)?
- [ ] Does it include a t-test of difference?
- [ ] Is the code readable with comments?
- [ ] Could you run it in Stata right now?

**Step 3: Iterate.** Ask Claude Code for two modifications:

1. *"Add a row for `rep78` (repair record). Handle the missing values in that variable."*
2. *"Add variable labels to the table so it shows 'Price (USD)' instead of 'price'."*

**Step 4: Evaluate.** Did Claude Code modify the do-file without breaking the existing structure? Did it handle the missing values in `rep78` correctly?

::: {.callout-tip collapse="true" appearance="simple"}
## Reveal: Strong Output Characteristics

- Loads `sysuse auto, clear`
- Loops over outcome variables to avoid repetitive code
- Uses `eststo` and `esttab`, or `tabstat`, or `estpost` with `ttest`
- Handles missing values in `rep78` explicitly
- Variable labels applied for display
- Code is well-commented and runnable
- No hard-coded paths (uses `auto` which needs no path)
:::

---

### Quick Reference

::::: {.two-col}
:::: {.col-left}

**Common interactions:**

| What You Want | What to Type |
|---------------|-------------|
| Start Claude Code | `claude` in the terminal |
| Plan before acting | `/plan` or *"use plan mode"* |
| Edit a file | *"Edit [path] to [change]"* |
| Create a file | *"Create a do-file that [spec]"* |
| Debug an error | Paste the log and ask *"What is wrong?"* |
| Exit | `/exit` or `Ctrl+C` |

::::
:::: {.col-right}

**Safe vs. unsafe at a glance:**

:::{.card .card-safe}
#### ✅ Safe
- "Write a do-file that..."
- "Debug this log output..."
- "Explain this Stata command..."
- "Generate a LaTeX table for..."
:::

:::{.card .card-danger}
#### ❌ Never
- "Read the raw survey data..."
- "Show me observations where name =..."
- "List all respondent names..."
- "Open the file on D: drive..."
:::

::::
:::::

---

### Next Steps

::::: {.grid}
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### 🟢 Start Here
Ask Claude Code to **explain** an unfamiliar section of an existing do-file.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### 🔵 Then Try
Paste a **Stata error** from a log and ask for a diagnosis.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### 🟣 Build Up
Ask Claude Code to **review** a do-file for coding standard violations.
:::
::::
:::: {.g-col-6}
:::{.card}
### 🟠 Go Deeper
Work through the [Blattman AI guide](https://claudeblattman.com/) for advanced workflows, custom skills, and MCP integrations.
:::
::::
:::::

**Resources:**

- [IPA AI Usage Guidelines](https://ipastorage.box.com/s/mvr67ygvz1y3v8qmgjey67lk7msmyeks) (required reading for all IPA AI tool use)
- [Blattman AI Guide](https://claudeblattman.com/) (recommended for going deeper)
- [Claude Code documentation](https://docs.anthropic.com/en/docs/claude-code/overview)
- [IPA Stata template](https://github.com/PovertyAction/ipa-stata-template)
- MIST helpdesk for AI use case approvals or questions

::: {.callout-note appearance="simple"}
Claude Code is a tool, not a replacement for analytical judgment. Always review code it generates, verify statistical choices, and confirm outputs before sharing. You remain responsible for the accuracy and quality of your work, even when AI helped create it.
:::

:::
